explanation: Ampersand separator runs dir after the original command. The & operator chains commands in cmd.exe regardless of the first command's exit code.
Identity disclosure via whoami. Reveals the Windows user context the application runs under, indicating privilege level.
Network configuration enumeration. ipconfig reveals IP addresses, subnet masks, and default gateways, useful for network reconnaissance.
User account enumeration. Lists all local user accounts, revealing the system's user configuration.
Administrator group enumeration. Lists members of the local Administrators group, identifying high-privilege accounts.
System information disclosure. Returns OS version, patch level, architecture, domain info -- critical for identifying exploitable vulnerabilities.
Hosts file read via type command. Reveals custom DNS entries and internal hostnames configured on the server.
Win.ini file read. Classic file read target that confirms file system access. The file always exists on Windows systems.
Pipe separator. The output of the original command is piped to dir, but dir ignores stdin so both commands effectively execute.
Pipe-based identity check. The pipe operator runs whoami and its output replaces the original command output in the response.
Pipe-based network enumeration. ipconfig output appears in the response if the application displays command output.
OR operator. dir runs only if the first command fails. Useful when the original command is expected to fail or when testing different separator behaviors.
Conditional execution on failure. whoami runs when the first command returns a non-zero exit code.
AND operator. dir runs only if the first command succeeds. Tests command chaining with conditional execution.
Conditional execution on success. whoami runs when the first command returns exit code 0.
Semicolon separator. Works in PowerShell and some Windows command processors as a statement separator.
PowerShell semicolon chain. The semicolon separates commands in PowerShell, running whoami after the original command.
Newline injection. %0a is a line feed character which acts as a command separator in cmd.exe. The second line runs as a new command.
URL-encoded newline with whoami. The newline character separates commands when the input is not properly sanitized before shell execution.
Time-based detection via ping. Sends 5 ICMP packets which takes approximately 5 seconds. Response timing confirms injection without visible output.
Windows timeout command for delay. Waits 5 seconds. Confirms command injection via response timing when output is not visible.
Pipe-based timeout delay. The timeout causes a 5-second delay in the response, confirming injection.
Process enumeration. Lists all running processes, revealing installed software, security products, and other services.
Environment variable disclosure. Dumps all environment variables which may contain paths, credentials, API keys, or configuration values.
Username via environment variable. %USERNAME% is expanded by cmd.exe to the current user's name, confirming injection and user context.
